content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 9, 1988

Richard Mastain
Executive Secretary
Commission on Teacher Credentialing
1812 9th Street
Sacramento, CA  94244-2700

RE:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-88-315

Dear Mr. Mastain:

     You have requested advice regarding the lobbying disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974.1/

QUESTION

     May voting members of the Commission on Teacher
Credentialing (CTC), its Executive Secretary, and members of
the management and professional staff of CTC attend a dinner at
which the per person cost will exceed $10, if the dinner is
arranged and paid for by a lobbyist employer which is
registered to lobby CTC?

CONCLUSION

     All voting members and employees of CTC may attend the
dinner, as long as the sponsor's lobbyist has not invited the
members and employees to attend the dinner, does not pay for
the dinner, is not the only representative of the lobbyist
employer at the dinner, does not solicit responses from the
members of employees, and is not the contact person for the
requested RSVP.

FACTS

     The members and employees of CTC have been invited to a
dinner by a lobbyist employer which has identified CTC as a
state agency whose administrative actions it attempts to
influence.  The cost of dinner per person will exceed $10.

_________________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
indicated.  Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code
of Regulations Section 18000, et seq.  All references to
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code
of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Mastain, Richard K.
Page Two

ANALYSIS

There is nothing in the Act which prohibits or limits gifts
from lobbyist employers to state agency officials or
employees. However, Section 86203 prohibits lobbyists or
lobbying firms from making gifts to one person aggregating more
than ten dollars in a calendar month, or from acting as an
agent or intermediary in the making of any gift, or arranging
for the making of any gift by any other person.

In addition, Section 86204 makes it a violation for state
agency officials to receive gifts prohibited by Section 86203.

Gift, as used in Section 86203, means a gift made directly
or indirectly to any state candidate, elected state officer, or
legislative official, or to any agency official of any agency
required to be listed on the registration statement of the
lobbying firm or the lobbyist employer of the lobbyist.
(Section 86201.)

A lobbyist "arranges" for the making of a gift if he or she:

1) Delivers a gift to the recipient.
2) Acts as the representative of the donor, if the donor
   is not present at the occasion of a gift. This does
   not include accompanying the recipient to an event
   where the donor will be present.
3) Invites or sends an invitation to an intended recipient
   regarding the occasion of a gift.
4) Solicits responses from an intended recipient
   concerning his or her attendance or nonattendance at
   the occasion of a gift.
5) Is designated as the representative of the donor to
   receive responses from an intended recipient concerning
   his or her attendance or nonattendance at the occasion
   of a gift.
6) Acts as an intermediary in connection with the reim-
   bursement of a recipient's expenses.

(Regulation 18624.)

Therefore, members and employees of CTC may attend a dinner
at which they will receive gifts aggregating more than $10 as
long as the sponsor's lobbyist does not make or arrange for the
making of the gifts.

Please note that members and employees of CTC are also
subject to the Act's conflict of interest disclosure and
disqualification provisions (Sections 87100-87313). Enclosed
is a copy of the Act and an FPPC publication regarding the
conflict of interest provisions. In addition, you may wish to
review your agency's Statement of Incompatible Activities.

Mastain, Richard K.
Page Three


     If you need additional information, please contact me at
(916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Diane M. Griffiths
                                        General Counsel
                                        [signature]

By:     Kevin S. Braaten-Moen
        Political Reform Consultant

STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor


COMMISSION ON TEACHER CREDENTIALING
1812 9TH STREET
SACRAMENTO, CA   94244-2700


August 8, 1988


Fair Political Practices Commission
Diane Griffiths, General Counsel
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA   95814

Dear Ms. Griffiths:

The Commission on Teacher Credentialing has directed me to ask for your
opinion on the application of the Fair Political Practices Act to the following
hypothetical state of facts:

The Commission on Teacher Credentialing is a statutory agency of state
government having jurisdiction to set standards for teacher education and to
grant and, to revoke credentials authorizing qualified persons to perform
instructional and other education-related services in the public schools.  Its
policy making body consists of seventeen voting members and five ex-officio
members.  It appoints an exempt Executive Secretary who in turn appoints
subordinate management, professional, and other staff.    The agency meets
monthly to enact administrative regulations, to administer professional
discipline, to approve teacher education programs, and to conduct other
related business.

An organization which consists of, and which represents, persons holding
credentials issued by the Commission on Teacher Credentialing, and which
organization has identified the Commission on Teacher Credentialing as a state
agency whose administrative or legislative actions it will attempt to influence,
has issued an invitation to the Commission, its Executive Secretary, the
members of the management staff, and selected professional employees to
attend a dinner at the expense of the organization.    In the terms of the
invitation,

"This (annual) event provides an opportunity for the leadership
of (the lobbying organization) to meet with members and staff of
the Commission on Teacher Credentialing.   We look forward to
renewing old acquaintances and getting to know new members".

Invitees are requested to R.S.V.P. through the Secretary of the Commission on
Teacher Credentialing at the office of the Commission.   The dinner is to be held
in a private dining room of a local restaurant.   The value of the dinner and
pre-dinner cocktails will substantially exceed ten dollars for each invitee, and
will be attended by a majority or more of the voting members of the
Commission, by its appointed Executive Secretary, and by several members of
management and professional staff of the CTC.

The Commission on Teacher Credentialing wishes to inquire as to whether
attendance at an "event" such as that described above on an annual or periodic
or any other basis, is in any way inappropriate under the Fair Political
Practices Act.

Sincerely,

[Signature]

Richard K. Mastain, Ed.D.
Executive Secretary

STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor

COMMISSION ON TEACHER CREDENTIALING
1812 9TH STREET
SACRAMENTO, CA  94244-2700


August 8, 1988


Fair Political Practices Commission
Diane Griffiths, General Counsel
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Ms. Griffiths:

The Commission on Teacher Credentialing has directed me to ask for your
opinion on the application of the Fair Political Practices Act to the following
hypothetical state of facts:

The Commission on Teacher Credentialing is a statutory agency of state
government having jurisdiction to set standards for teacher education and to
grant and to revoke credentials authorizing qualified persons to perform
instructional and other education-related services in the public schools.  Its
policy making body consists of seventeen voting members and five ex-officio
members.  It appoints an exempt Executive Secretary who in turn appoints
subordinate management, professional, and other staff.  The agency meets
monthly to enact administrative regulations, to administer professional
discipline, to approve teacher education programs, and to conduct other
related business.

An organization which consists of, and which represents, persons holding
credentials issued by the Commission on Teacher Credentialing, and which
organization has identified the Commission on Teacher Credentialing as a state
agency whose administrative or legislative actions it will attempt to influence,
has issued an invitation to the Commission, its Executive Secretary, the
members of the management staff, and selected professional employees to
attend a dinner at the expense of the organization.  In the terms of the
invitation:

"This (annual) event provides an opportunity for the leadership
of (the lobbying organization) to meet with members and staff of
the Commission on Teacher Credentialing.  We look forward to
renewing old acquaintances and getting to know new members".

Invitees are requested to R.S.V.P. through the Secretary of the Commission on
Teacher Credentialing at the office of the Commission.  The dinner is to be held
in a private dining room of a local restaurant.  The value of the dinner and
pre-dinner cocktails will substantially exceed ten dollars for each invitee, and
will be attended by a majority or more of the voting members of the
Commission, by its appointed Executive Secretary, and several members of the
management and professional staff of the CTC.

The Commission on Teacher Credentialing wishes to inquire as to whether
attendance at an "event" such as that described above on an annual or periodic
or any other basis, is in any way inappropriate under the Fair Political
Practices Act.

Sincerely,

[Signature]

Richard K. Mastain, Ed.D.
Executive Secretary
